skill.name: colorize
skill.description: Add strategic color to features that are too monochromatic or lack visual interest. Makes interfaces more engaging and expressive.
skill.summary: Transform monochromatic designs into visually engaging interfaces with strategic, purposeful color application.
skill.author: pbakaus

security_audit.summary: All static analysis findings are false positives. The detected patterns (external_commands, blocker, env_access) appear in documentation text, not executable code. This skill contains only instructional content for UI color design - no code execution, network requests, or filesystem operations. Safe for publication.

content.user_title: Add Strategic Color to Your Designs
content.value_statement: Transform gray, lifeless interfaces into warm, engaging experiences. This skill applies purposeful color theory to improve visual hierarchy and user delight.
actual_capabilities: Analyze existing designs for color opportunities and missing visual interest
Apply semantic color for states (success, error, warning, info)
Create balanced color palettes using the 60-30-10 rule
Implement accessible color combinations meeting WCAG contrast requirements
Add color to interactive elements (buttons, links, hover states, focus rings)
limitations: Requires existing design context and brand colors from the codebase
Cannot override established brand guidelines without user approval
Color decisions require user feedback for subjective preferences
Accessibility testing should be verified with actual tools for critical applications

use_cases.title: Enhance Dashboard Visual Appeal
use_cases.description: Add strategic color to a monochromatic admin dashboard, improving data visualization and status indicators while maintaining professional appearance.

use_cases.title: Brand Integration for Startup MVP
use_cases.description: Apply startup brand colors consistently across a grayscale prototype, creating visual hierarchy and emotional connection with early users.

use_cases.title: Improve SaaS Component Library
use_cases.description: Introduce semantic color system to a design system, ensuring consistent state indicators and accessible interactive elements.
use_cases.target_user: Design system maintainers and UI engineers

prompt_templates.title: Basic Color Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the current design of [component/page]. Identify areas where color could improve visual hierarchy, communicate meaning, or add delight. List specific opportunities and explain the purpose of each color addition.

prompt_templates.title: Apply Brand Colors
prompt_templates.prompt: Our brand colors are [list colors]. Apply these consistently across [feature], following the 60-30-10 rule. Ensure WCAG contrast compliance and explain your application strategy.

prompt_templates.title: Semantic Color System
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a semantic color system for [application type]. Define colors for success, error, warning, and info states. Show how each applies to components like badges, alerts, and status indicators.

prompt_templates.title: Full Color Transformation
prompt_templates.prompt: Transform this monochromatic [interface] into a warm, engaging experience. Use OKLCH for color definitions. Apply color across backgrounds, typography, borders, and interactive states. Justify each choice and verify accessibility.

output_examples.input: A grayscale task management dashboard with no color beyond black, white, and gray.
output: Added emerald green success indicators for completed tasks
Applied amber warning badges for approaching deadlines
Introduced subtle warm background tint (oklch(97% 0.01 60)) to replace pure gray
Colored primary CTA buttons with brand blue for visual hierarchy

output_examples.input: A SaaS settings page with pure gray borders and neutral text.
output: Replaced gray dividers with subtle colored borders matching brand palette
Added colored left-border accents to active settings sections
Applied brand color to focus rings for improved accessibility
Used colored icons to improve section recognition

best_practices: Always define color purpose before application - semantic meaning beats decoration
Use OKLCH color space for perceptually uniform color scales and better harmony
Test all color combinations for WCAG contrast compliance and color blindness accessibility
anti_patterns: Using purple-blue gradients by default - this is generic AI aesthetic, not intentional design
Applying color randomly without semantic meaning or consistent system
Relying on color alone to communicate information - always add icons or labels

faq.question: How many colors should I use in my design?
faq.answer: Follow the 60-30-10 rule: one dominant color (60%), one secondary color (30%), and one accent color (10%). Beyond neutrals, limit yourself to 2-4 colors maximum for cohesion.

faq.question: What is OKLCH and why should I use it?
faq.answer: OKLCH is a perceptually uniform color space where equal numerical steps appear equal to the human eye. This makes generating harmonious color scales intuitive and accessible.

faq.question: How do I ensure color accessibility?
faq.answer: Maintain 4.5:1 contrast ratio for text and 3:1 for UI components. Never use color as the only indicator. Test with color blindness simulators for red-green combinations.

faq.question: Should I use pure black (#000) for text?
faq.answer: No. Pure black on white creates harsh contrast. Use dark gray with subtle color tint (like oklch(20% 0.01 250)) for a more sophisticated, comfortable reading experience.

faq.question: How do I handle neutrals without looking gray and lifeless?
faq.answer: Replace pure grays with tinted neutrals. Add subtle warm (yellow/orange) or cool (blue) undertones to create warmth while maintaining neutral appearance.

faq.question: What if my client wants every section to be colorful?
faq.answer: Explain that strategic restraint creates impact. Color everywhere means nothing stands out. Show them the 60-30-10 rule and how hierarchy guides user attention effectively.
